perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of ten (10) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement's duration to ten years may be problematic, as California law imposes limits on the enforceability of long-term contracts, particularly those that restrain trade or competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally voids contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While a confidentiality agreement is not a direct restraint on trade, a 10-year duration could be deemed unreasonable and thus an unlawful restraint, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated or irrelevant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text extends the agreement's duration from three to ten years, which potentially violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The Law Explanation highlights that a 10-year duration for a confidentiality agreement could be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade if considered unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While a confidentiality agreement is not a direct restraint, a long duration can create an unreasonable restraint. Thus, the extended duration creates a contradiction. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition is met because the change sets a deadline that violates state law, potentially making the term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice, provided however, that Paragraphs 3, 4, 5, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon ten (10) days written notice, provided however, that Paragraphs 3, 4, 5, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days might conflict with legal principles regarding reasonable notice, especially if the agreement involves significant ongoing obligations. It could be considered insufficient time for either party to wind down business relations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While section 1946 primarily concerns real property, it establishes a precedent for notice periods in contractual settings. Courts may apply similar reasoning to other contracts requiring reasonable notice, and 10 days may be deemed insufficient for a complex confidentiality agreement, rendering the termination clause potentially unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. HIRING [1925 - 1997.270] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Hiring of Real Property [1940 - 1954.07] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1946. (a) A hiring of real property, for a term not specified by the parties, is deemed to be renewed as stated in Section 1945, at the end of the term implied by law unless one of the parties gives written notice to the other of that party’s intention to terminate the same, at least as long before the expiration thereof as the term of the hiring itself, not exceeding 30 days; provided, however, that as to tenancies from month to month either of the parties may terminate the same by giving at least 30 days’ written notice thereof at any time and the rent shall be due and payable to and including the date of termination. It shall be competent for the parties to provide by an agreement at the time the tenancy is created that a notice of the intention to terminate the same may be given at any time not less than seven days before the expiration of the term thereof. The notice herein required shall be given in the manner pr
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 30-day written notice for termination, while the changed text reduces this to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this might conflict with 'reasonable notice' principles, especially if significant obligations are involved. The Law Explanation, referring to CIV 1946 regarding real property, establishes a precedent for notice periods, suggesting that courts may apply similar reasoning to other contracts requiring reasonable notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 1946) states that for real property, notice must be 'at least as long before the expiration thereof as the term of the hiring itself, not exceeding 30 days'. Although this snippet explicitly concerns real property, the Law Explanation suggests that the courts may apply similar reasoning to other contracts requiring reasonable notice, and ten days notice is likely not a reasonable amount of time, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as such further equitable relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, and waives any right to contest this.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase 'waives any right to contest this' may be unenforceable. In California, waivers of legal rights must be knowing and voluntary. A blanket waiver without specific consideration or explanation could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, especially if it deprives the Recipient of due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3513 states that anyone may waive the benefit of a law intended solely for his benefit, but a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement. The right to a fair legal process is arguably a matter of public policy. Making the waiver uncontestable is against public interest.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 4. MAXIMS OF JURISPRUDENCE [3509 - 3548] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) 3513. Any one may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for their benefit. But a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement. (Amended by Stats. 2024, Ch. 812, Sec. 2. (AB 1899) Effective January 1, 2025.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text adds 'waives any right to contest this', which contradicts California Civil Code Section 3513. The code states that while one may waive a law intended solely for their benefit, a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement. The Law Explanation argues the right to a fair legal process is a matter of public policy, making the waiver uncontestable against public interest. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by potentially contravening a law established for a public reason through a private agreement, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1.
